perturbation.original_text: Without the prior written consent of Fox, (i) neither Company nor those of its Representatives who are aware of the Confidential Information of Fox will initiate or cause to be initiated any communications with any employee of Fox concerning the Confidential Information, and (ii) none of Company's directors, officers or employees who are aware of the Confidential Information will, for the two-year period from the date of the Agreement, solicit or cause to be solicited for employment any employee of Fox who is aware of the Confidential Information of Fox.
perturbation.changed_text: Without the prior written consent of Fox, (i) neither Company nor those of its Representatives who are aware of the Confidential Information of Fox will initiate or cause to be initiated any communications with any employee of Fox concerning the Confidential Information, and (ii) none of Company's directors, officers or employees who are aware of the Confidential Information will, for the five-year period from the date of the Agreement, solicit or cause to be solicited for employment any employee of Fox who is aware of the Confidential Information of Fox.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly in California, potentially conflicting with laws regarding employee mobility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow exceptions exist (e.g., sale of goodwill), a blanket five-year non-solicitation agreement might be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade, especially if it unduly restricts employee mobility and opportunities. The original two-year period is already potentially problematic, extending it to five years exacerbates the issue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from two to five years, which creates a contradiction with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. This section, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The five-year non-solicitation agreement restrains employees from engaging in their profession by limiting their ability to seek employment elsewhere. The extended duration exacerbates the restriction and increases the likelihood of it being deemed an unlawful restraint of trade, making the contractual term unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested or required by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, it is agreed that the Receiving Party or its Representative, as the case may be, will provide the Furnishing Party with prompt notice of such request(s) so that it may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance by the Receiving Party or its Representative with the provisions of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested or required by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, it is agreed that the Receiving Party or its Representative, as the case may be, will provide the Furnishing Party with notice of such request(s) only after disclosing the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to require notice only *after* disclosure could violate legal requirements for pre-disclosure notification, particularly in cases involving legally protected information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under HIPAA, covered entities are required to make reasonable efforts to limit the Protected Health Information (PHI) disclosed to that which is minimally necessary. Premature disclosure of confidential information, including PHI, without providing the furnishing party an opportunity to obtain a protective order or seek other remedies would violate HIPAA regulations regarding minimizing disclosure and potentially breach patient confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a legal contradiction by requiring notice to the Furnishing Party *after* the disclosure of confidential information, instead of before. This directly undermines the purpose of allowing the Furnishing Party to seek a protective order or other remedy *prior* to the disclosure, as described in the original text. The Law Explanation highlights that premature disclosure, especially of PHI under HIPAA, violates regulations aimed at minimizing disclosure. Snippet 1, although primarily a navigation aid for the eCFR, confirms that Title 45 contains regulations pertaining to uses and disclosures where authorization might not be required. This context, combined with the Perturbation and Law Explanations, indicates that delaying notification until *after* disclosure contradicts legal requirements for controlled information releases.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware and excluding conflict of laws principles could potentially affect the enforceability of specific clauses, particularly concerning non-competes or non-solicitation agreements, as California law is generally more protective of employee rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 invalidates contracts that restrain trade. By choosing Delaware law and excluding its conflict of laws principles, the contract attempts to bypass California's strong public policy against non-compete agreements, which could render certain clauses unenforceable if a California court determines that California law should apply despite the choice of law provision.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Delaware while excluding conflict of laws principles. This creates a contradiction with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as highlighted in both scraped law snippets. Section 16600(a) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." By selecting Delaware law and excluding conflict of law principles, the contract attempts to circumvent California's prohibition on non-compete agreements. This directly undermines the legal protection afforded by California law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon providing the Receiving Party with a specific list of the Confidential Information to be returned to the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly deliver to the Furnishing Party or its Representatives, the Confidential Information listed, together with all copies thereof, in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Representatives, which was actually furnished by the Furnishing Party to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon providing the Receiving Party with a specific list of the Confidential Information to be returned to the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party will deliver to the Furnishing Party or its Representatives, the Confidential Information listed, together with all copies thereof, in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Representatives, which was actually furnished by the Furnishing Party to the Receiving Party within 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to a fixed 90-day period for returning confidential information after a request could violate regulations requiring quicker action with certain types of sensitive data, such as personal data under privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA grants consumers the right to request deletion of their personal information. While not directly about returning confidential information between businesses, the principle of timely action is relevant. A 90-day period to comply with returning or deleting personal data following a specific request could be considered unreasonable and non-compliant with the spirit and requirements of CCPA, which requires businesses to comply with consumer requests 'as soon as feasible.'

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.130. Notice, Disclosure, Correction, and Deletion Requirements (a) In order to comply with Sections 1798.100, 1798.105, 1798.106, 1798.110, 1798.115, and 1798.125, a business shall, in a form that is reasonably accessible to consumers: (1) (A) Make available to consumers two or more designated methods for submitting requests for information required to be disclosed pursuant to Sections 1798.110 and 1798.115, or requests for deletion or correction pursuant to Sections 1798.105 and 1798.106, respectively, including, at a minimum, a toll-free telephone number. A business that operates exclusively online and has a direct relationship with a consumer from whom it collects personal information shall only be required to provide an email address for submitting requests for information required to be disclosed pursuant to Sections 1798.110 and 1798.115, or for requests for deletion or correction pursuant to Sections 1798.105 and
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correct : You may ask businesses to correct inaccurate information that they have about you. Right to limit use and disclosure of sensitive personal information : You can direct businesses to only use your sensitive personal information (for example, your social security number, financial account information, your precise geolocation data, or your genetic data) for limited purposes, such as providing you with the services you requested. You also have the right to be notified, before or at the point businesses collect your personal information, of the types of personal information they are collecting and what they may do with that information. Generally, businesses cannot discriminate against you for exercising your rights under the CCPA. Businesses cannot make you waive these rights, and any contract provision that says you waive these rights is unenforceable. 2. What if I am not a California resident? Only California residents have rights under the CCPA. A California resident is a natural person (as opposed to a corporation or other business entity) who resides in California, even if the person is temporarily outside of the state. 3. What is considered personal information and sensitive personal information under the CCPA? Personal
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'promptly' with a fixed 90-day period for returning confidential information. The Law Explanation states this 'could be considered unreasonable and non-compliant with the spirit and requirements of CCPA, which requires businesses to comply with consumer requests 'as soon as feasible.'' Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that consumers have the right to request deletion of personal information and businesses must respond to such requests. By setting a 90-day deadline, the changed text potentially violates the requirement to respond 'as soon as feasible', creating a contradiction.
